topics.label: Safety, Assurance & Governance
topics.focus: This topic has two pillars with UNEQUAL weight. Pillar 1 is the PRIMARY focus. PILLAR 1 (PRIMARY — aim for 2 of 3 Key Developments): AI SAFETY RESEARCH & ASSURANCE — alignment research (RLHF, constitutional AI, scalable oversight, weak-to-strong generalization); interpretability and mechanistic interpretability advances; red-teaming methodologies and adversarial robustness; dangerous capability evaluations (cyber, biological, persuasion, autonomous replication) from AISI, METR, Apollo Research, and lab-internal teams; evaluation integrity and benchmark gaming; evaluation methodology research (evaluator validity, LLM-as-judge reliability, eval design); agentic supervision and control (monitoring, kill switches, CoT faithfulness, sandboxing); jailbreak research and mitigation; responsible AI tooling (guardrails, content filtering, bias detection); AI auditing methodologies and assurance tooling; and safety benchmarks. Search arXiv, lab blogs (Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepMind, METR, MIRI, Redwood Research), and safety evaluation bodies (AISI, Apollo Research, CAIS) explicitly. PILLAR 2 (SECONDARY — at most 1 of 3 Key Developments): GOVERNANCE & REGULATION — only include legislation or regulation as a Key Development if it sets a genuinely new precedent (e.g., first law of its kind, first enforcement action, new compliance deadline). Routine legislative movement (committee votes, session scheduling, bill amendments, incremental trilogue progress) belongs in Landscape Trends, not Key Developments. Cover: formal AI governance frameworks (EU AI Act enforcement, NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001); multi-jurisdiction regulatory developments; enterprise risk management for LLM deployments; audit-stable telemetry and observability schema compliance. BALANCE RULE: At least 2 of 3 Key Developments MUST come from Pillar 1 (safety research, evaluations, techniques, tooling). If web search returns mostly regulatory news, you have not searched hard enough for safety research — go back and run dedicated searches for lab blogs, AISI, METR, Apollo Research, arXiv safety papers before finalizing.

critic_rules.name: Pillar Balance
critic_rules.check: At least 2 of 3 Key Developments MUST come from Pillar 1 (safety research, evaluations, capability assessments, alignment techniques, guardrails, agentic supervision — NOT legislation or regulation). Routine legislative movement (committee votes, session scheduling, bill amendments, trilogue progress) should not be a Key Development unless it sets a genuinely new precedent. Flag if fewer than 2 Key Developments are from Pillar 1, or if more than 1 is routine governance/legislation.

topics.label: Enterprise GenAI Adoption
topics.focus: how enterprises are deploying GenAI at scale including specific case studies with ROI data, productivity metrics, and failure modes; organizational and workforce change management; build-vs-buy and vendor selection decisions; large-scale regulated industry adoption (financial services, healthcare, legal, government); barriers to production deployment; standards adoption exposure — telemetry/observability standards stability (e.g., OTel GenAI semconv status) and its procurement and migration implications; and analyst forecasts from IDC, Gartner, Forrester, and McKinsey on enterprise AI spend and readiness

topics.label: Agentic Systems
topics.focus: Agent frameworks and agent-specific system design: autonomous agent frameworks (LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, Mastra, etc.) and their production readiness; multi-agent coordination protocols (MCP, A2A) and ecosystem adoption; tool use, planning, delegation, long-horizon task reliability, and memory architectures (episodic, semantic, procedural) for persistent agent state; agent evaluation benchmarks and failure-mode taxonomies; agentic workflow design patterns; and AgentOps only where the development is specifically about agent behavior, orchestration, or non-deterministic agent evaluation. EXCLUDE FROM THIS TOPIC — generic LLM observability platforms, general-purpose eval tools, serving-framework releases, inference optimization, and broad production infrastructure unless the primary significance is clearly agent-specific (for example: tool-use tracing for agents, multi-agent orchestration, agent memory management, or agent protocol adoption). If the same item would read unchanged in the LLM Production Infrastructure brief, it does not belong here.

topics.label: Models & Market
topics.focus: two distinct but related areas covered together: (1) FRONTIER CAPABILITIES — latest model releases and benchmark results from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Meta AI, Mistral, DeepSeek, xAI, and others; significant research breakthroughs; independent benchmark evaluations (SWE-bench, GPQA, ARC-AGI, MMMU); context window advances; reasoning and coding capability gaps; and (2) MARKET & PRODUCTS — commercial product launches, API changes, pricing shifts, platform integrations, and competitive positioning; the open-weight vs. closed model dynamic; enterprise platform updates from Microsoft, AWS, Google Cloud, Salesforce; and analyst views on market structure. Cover both lenses in each brief — capability without market context, or market without capability grounding, misses the full picture

topics.label: LLM Production Infrastructure
topics.focus: Infrastructure for businesses to RUN generative AI and agentic systems in production — the operate side. Distinct from Enterprise GenAI Adoption, which covers how businesses USE GenAI and manage transformation: this topic is the platform and infrastructure businesses operate; that topic is how the business deploys, governs change, and measures value. Pitch coverage at a reader who is both a senior ML engineer and a technology executive: technical depth is welcome at the tool, platform, and deployment-architecture level — not at the inference-engine implementation level. LEAD WITH (and use for Technical Deep-Dive) — observability, monitoring, and evaluation platforms for production GenAI and agentic systems (Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, LangSmith): releases and operational capabilities; managed model-hosting and fine-tuning services (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Together, Fireworks, Modal, Baseten): pricing, SLAs, capability and API changes; the serving stack at the selection level — vLLM/SGLang/TGI/TensorRT-LLM releases and their operational trade-offs for hosting choices; self-hosting economics (utilization-aware cost models, make-vs-buy, GPU capacity planning); deployment patterns for regulated environments (on-prem, sovereign, VPC isolation). ALSO COVER (single Key Development bullets at most, never the Technical Deep-Dive) — inference-efficiency and serving research ONLY when it changes a near-term procurement, capacity, cost, or SLA decision: lead with the decision implication, keep mechanism to a sentence. If fewer than 2 pre-retrieved candidates are decision-relevant for this audience, include fewer — audience relevance outranks the scholarly quota. EXCLUDE FROM THIS TOPIC — inference-engine implementation research (scheduler algorithms, KV-cache transfer optimization, MoE routing internals, parallelism switching mechanics) with no direct platform-decision consequence; academic studies of evaluation methodology and evaluator validity (those belong in Safety, Assurance & Governance); telemetry standards status and governance such as OTel semconv stability status (adoption/migration exposure belongs in Enterprise GenAI Adoption, audit/compliance implications in Safety & Governance); AI safety research, alignment, and regulation (Safety & Governance); model capability and benchmark results (Models & Market); agent frameworks and orchestration (Agentic Systems); GPU supply, data center buildout, and sovereign-compute policy (AI Infrastructure & Geopolitics owns the macro supply and geopolitics story — this topic owns running models on infrastructure you or your vendor already have)

rejection_filters: Reject inference-engine implementation research (scheduling algorithms, KV-cache transfer, MoE routing internals, parallelism mechanics) unless it changes a procurement, capacity, cost, or SLA decision — and then cover the decision implication in one bullet with mechanism kept to a sentence. It must never be the Technical Deep-Dive subject.

Reject standards-status updates and unresolved-gap restatements (e.g., a spec still in Development status) unless a concrete change occurred this cycle (spec release, stability promotion, breaking schema change).
Reject vendor maintenance-only releases (bugfix changelogs with no new operational capability) from all sections including Vendor Landscape.
Reject academic evaluation-methodology studies (evaluator validity, LLM-as-judge reliability, eval design) unless the core novelty is an operational platform capability.

topics.label: AI Infrastructure & Geopolitics
topics.focus: the physical and geopolitical substrate that determines AI capacity: semiconductor supply chains (TSMC, ASML, HBM memory, CoWoS packaging) and disruption risks; GPU and TPU availability, allocation, and waitlists; energy supply for AI — power consumption trends, data center energy sourcing, nuclear and renewable commitments by hyperscalers; new data center announcements and buildout timelines; sovereign compute initiatives and hyperscaler regional expansion, with particular focus on Australia and Asia-Pacific (when will AWS, Azure, Google, Oracle put meaningful GPU capacity into Australian sovereign territory?); US and allied export controls on advanced chips and their downstream effects on training and inference capacity; geopolitical risks to the AI supply chain (Taiwan, China, trade policy); and macroeconomic factors affecting AI infrastructure investment. Preferred sources: Epoch AI, SemiAnalysis, The Information, IEEE Spectrum, official hyperscaler infrastructure announcements, and energy/policy outlets
